source_intake_record.raw_question_text: As shown in the figure, in $\triangle ABC$, $AB=AC=2\sqrt{5}$, $\angle BAC=\alpha^\circ$, $\tan\angle ABC=\frac{1}{2}$, $G$ is the midpoint of $BC$, and $D$ is a moving point in the plane with $DG=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{5}$. If line segment $BD$ rotates counterclockwise by $\alpha^\circ$ around point $D$ to obtain $DB'$, what is the maximum area of quadrilateral $BACB'$?
source_intake_record.raw_answer_text:
\textbf{Solution:} As shown in the figure, connect AD, AG, and draw GH perpendicular to AB at point H through point G,\\
since $AB=AC=2\sqrt{5}$ and $BG=GC$,\\
thus, $AG\perp BC$,\\
since $\tan\angle ABC=\frac{AG}{BG}=\frac{1}{2}$,\\
thus, $AG=2$ and $BG=4$,\\
since $\sin\angle ABG=\sin\angle GBH$,\\
thus, $\frac{GH}{BC}=\frac{AG}{AB}$,\\
thus, $\frac{GH}{4}=\frac{2}{2\sqrt{5}}$,\\
thus, $GH=\frac{4\sqrt{5}}{5}$,\\
since $AB=AC$, $DB=DB'$, $\angle BAC=\angle BDB'$,\\
thus, $\angle ABC=\angle DBB'$, $\frac{AB}{BC}=\frac{BD}{BB'}$,\\
thus, $\angle ABD=\angle CBB'$,\\
thus, $\triangle ABD\sim \triangle CBB'$,\\
thus, $\frac{S_{\triangle ABD}}{S_{\triangle CBB'}}=\left(\frac{AB}{BC}\right)^2=\left(\frac{2\sqrt{5}}{4}\right)^2=\frac{5}{16}$,\\
since $DG=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{5}$,\\
thus, the motion trajectory of point G is a circle with G as the center and $\frac{\sqrt{5}}{5}$ as the radius. When point D is on the extension line of HG, the area of $\triangle ABD$ is maximized, with the maximum value $=\frac{1}{2}\times 2\sqrt{5}\times \left(\frac{4\sqrt{5}}{5}+\frac{\sqrt{5}}{5}\right)=5$,\\
thus, the maximum area of $\triangle BCB'$ is $16$,\\
thus, the maximum area of quadrilateral $BACB'$ is $\frac{1}{2}\times 8\times 2+16=\boxed{24}$.

thus, $\frac{S_{\triangle ABD}}{S_{\triangle CBB'}}=\left(\frac{AB}{BC}\right)^2=\left(\frac{2\sqrt{5}}{4}\right)^2=\frac{5}{16}$,\\
since $DG=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{5}$,\\
thus, the motion trajectory of point G is a circle with G as the center and $\frac{\sqrt{5}}{5}$ as the radius. When point D is on the extension line of HG, the area of $\triangle ABD$ is maximized, with the maximum value $=\frac{1}{2}\times 2\sqrt{5}\times \left(\frac{4\sqrt{5}}{5}+\frac{\sqrt{5}}{5}\right)=5$,\\
thus, the maximum area of $\triangle BCB'$ is $16$,\\
thus, the maximum area of quadrilateral $BACB'$ is $\frac{1}{2}\times 8\times 2+16=\boxed{24}$

If line segment $BD$ rotates counterclockwise by $\alpha^\circ$ around point $D$ to obtain $DB'$, what is the maximum area of quadrilateral $BACB'$?

normalized_value.target_text: If line segment $BD$ rotates counterclockwise by $\alpha^\circ$ around point $D$ to obtain $DB'$, what is the maximum area of quadrilateral $BACB'$?
normalized_value.expected_answer:
\textbf{Solution:} As shown in the figure, connect AD, AG, and draw GH perpendicular to AB at point H through point G,\\
since $AB=AC=2\sqrt{5}$ and $BG=GC$,\\
thus, $AG\perp BC$,\\
since $\tan\angle ABC=\frac{AG}{BG}=\frac{1}{2}$,\\
thus, $AG=2$ and $BG=4$,\\
since $\sin\angle ABG=\sin\angle GBH$,\\
thus, $\frac{GH}{BC}=\frac{AG}{AB}$,\\
thus, $\frac{GH}{4}=\frac{2}{2\sqrt{5}}$,\\
thus, $GH=\frac{4\sqrt{5}}{5}$,\\
since $AB=AC$, $DB=DB'$, $\angle BAC=\angle BDB'$,\\
thus, $\angle ABC=\angle DBB'$, $\frac{AB}{BC}=\frac{BD}{BB'}$,\\
thus, $\angle ABD=\angle CBB'$,\\
thus, $\triangle ABD\sim \triangle CBB'$,\\
thus, $\frac{S_{\triangle ABD}}{S_{\triangle CBB'}}=\left(\frac{AB}{BC}\right)^2=\left(\frac{2\sqrt{5}}{4}\right)^2=\frac{5}{16}$,\\
since $DG=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{5}$,\\
thus, the motion trajectory of point G is a circle with G as the center and $\frac{\sqrt{5}}{5}$ as the radius. When point D is on the extension line of HG, the area of $\triangle ABD$ is maximized, with the maximum value $=\frac{1}{2}\times 2\sqrt{5}\times \left(\frac{4\sqrt{5}}{5}+\frac{\sqrt{5}}{5}\right)=5$,\\
thus, the maximum area of $\triangle BCB'$ is $16$,\\
thus, the maximum area of quadrilateral $BACB'$ is $\frac{1}{2}\times 8\times 2+16=\boxed{24}$
